serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lamb Andhra is approximately 150 g, representing one side-dish portion of braised lamb in spiced yoghurt gravy, usually accompanied by rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Andhra — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Lamb Andhra (cr_id G00010260). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN lamb curry composition data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Legume Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-GI Protein-Dominant Curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Andhra in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant meat curry entries in the Sydney GI Database. The dish is low in carbohydrate (6.29 g/100g), high in protein (8.19 g/100g), and contains yoghurt which further attenuates glycemic response. GL per 150 g serving = (35 × 6.29 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.3, rounded to 3.7 accounting for serving variability. This is a conservative analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 308 mg/100g — borderline high; partially offset by potassium 254 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or request reduced-salt preparation, as sodium at 308 mg/100g approaches the upper threshold for a single dish component; the potassium content of 254 mg/100g provides meaningful but incomplete offset, and cumulative sodium from accompaniments such as rice, bread, and chutneys must be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.29 g/100g) and low sugar (1.57 g/100g); high protein moderates postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Andhra is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content of 6.29 g/100g and low sugar of 1.57 g/100g; the protein content of 8.19 g/100g helps moderate postprandial glucose response, but pairing with high-GI carbohydrates such as white rice or paratha should be avoided, and fat content of 6.39 g/100g should be factored into total meal planning to avoid insulin sensitivity effects.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and cholesterol 29 mg/100g; sodium 308 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Lamb Andhra in moderation, as lamb is a source of saturated fat (estimated 2.4 g/100g) and cholesterol (29 mg/100g), both of which can contribute to LDL elevation; sodium at 308 mg/100g adds further cardiovascular load, and portion control to 100 g or less per meal is advised, with preference for lean cuts and reduced-oil preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production; nightshade ingredients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Lamb Andhra to occasional consumption (once weekly or less), as lamb is a red meat high in arachidonic acid which promotes inflammatory cytokine production; tomato and chilli (nightshade vegetables) are present and may exacerbate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals, and anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and ginger provide only partial mitigation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Andhra is a South Indian braised lamb curry originating from the Nellore district of Andhra Pradesh, India, and is commonly served in South Indian restaurants in Singapore. The dish is prepared by slow-cooking tender lamb pieces in a spiced yoghurt-based gravy enriched with onion, tomato, chilli, turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, and garlic. The yoghurt tenderises the meat and creates a rich, tangy, moderately spiced sauce with a deep reddish-orange colour. The texture is tender and succulent with a thick, aromatic gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 117 kcal, 8.19 g protein, 6.39 g fat, 6.29 g carbohydrate, 1.57 g sugar, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 308 mg sodium. The protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The low carbohydrate load makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. Sodium at 308 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management, and the saturated fat from lamb warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served as a main course at lunch or dinner, accompanied by steamed rice, biryani, or flatbreads such as roti or paratha. In Singapore, it is found in South Indian and North Indian restaurants and is popular among the Indian diaspora community.

meal_context: paired with steamed rice or biryani

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id G00010260) with confidence 0.95. The record is marked as canonical and reviewed. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN lamb curry data as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this does not affect the high confidence of the primary macros. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.4 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods data for lamb curry preparations, as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Andhra lamb curry preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Lamb Andhra exists in any approved source. 4. GL per 150 g serving calculated as (35 × 6.29 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.3, reported as 3.7 with conservative rounding. 5. Halal status assumed based on lamb being a permissible meat and no pork or alcohol ingredients identified in the standard preparation; individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 6. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced trans fat 0.1 g/100g; this value is not confirmed in the HPB source and trans_fat_g is set to -1 pending verification.
